title: Machine Learning Fundamentals
description: Classical ML algorithms, evaluation metrics, model selection, leakage, and production tradeoffs.

questions.title: Compare precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC.
questions.scenario: A fraud model catches more fraud but blocks more legitimate transactions.
questions.expectedAnswer: Precision measures correctness among predicted positives. Recall measures coverage of actual positives. F1 balances precision and recall. ROC-AUC measures ranking ability across thresholds. The right metric depends on business cost of false positives and false negatives. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A fraud model catches more fraud but blocks more legitimate transactions. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Compare precision, recall, F1, and ROC-AUC.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real machine learning fundamentals setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: What is the difference between linear regression and logistic regression?
questions.scenario: You need to predict house price versus whether a customer will churn.
questions.expectedAnswer: Linear regression predicts continuous values by modeling a linear relationship. Logistic regression predicts class probabilities using a logistic function and is commonly used for binary classification. Candidates should discuss assumptions, loss functions, interpretability, and thresholding. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: Compare decision trees, random forests, and gradient boosting.
questions.scenario: A tabular risk model needs strong performance and reasonable explainability.
questions.expectedAnswer: Decision trees are interpretable but can overfit. Random forests reduce variance by averaging many trees. Gradient boosting builds trees sequentially to correct errors and often performs strongly on tabular data, but can require careful tuning and monitoring. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A tabular risk model needs strong performance and reasonable explainability. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: What is data leakage?
questions.scenario: A churn model uses a feature generated after the cancellation request.
questions.expectedAnswer: Data leakage happens when training features contain information that would not be available at prediction time or when validation data contaminates training. It produces inflated offline metrics and poor production performance. Detection involves time-based splits, feature audits, pipeline review, and suspicious metric investigation. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A churn model uses a feature generated after the cancellation request. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: How do you choose a model for a production ML problem?
questions.scenario: A team asks whether to use XGBoost, a neural network, or an LLM for tabular prediction.
questions.expectedAnswer: Choose based on metric fit, data size, feature types, interpretability, latency, cost, maintainability, robustness, fairness, and monitoring needs. Start with simple baselines, compare candidates on validation data, and select the simplest model that meets business and operational requirements. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.
